dialogue.utterance: 911 Unit 3, respond for a cardiac arrest in [CITY] after reported seizure.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened here today?
She had a seizure and became unresponsive after it. Struggling to talk during the fit.
Unresponsive to voice and pain. Eyes closed, and no movement detected.
Airway is patent. Nasopharyngeal airway is already in place with BVM assistance.
Respiratory effort absent. No breathing at the time of arrival.
No central pulse detected. RFD had been performing manual compressions.
NPA and BVM in place. First IO attempt failed, second successful in the tibia.
Blood pressure is 0/0. No measurable reading with manual palpation.
Pulse ox shows 0%.
You said she was taking seizure meds. Was she on her usual dose?
She had a seizure, then slumped over-unresponsive. No earlier symptoms.
Skin remains warm. No visible rashes, abrasions, or signs of trauma.
Glucose was 163 mg/dL upon arrival. No hypoglycemia identified.
Epinephrine 1mg 1:10,000 via tibial IO is being administered now.
Capnography waveform confirmed. Ventilation through ETT every 6 seconds.
Activated 4-lead ECG as per protocol. Asystole with brief episodes of PEA.
Intubated using a McGrath scope. Vocal cords were directly visualized.
Proceeding with cardiac arrest protocol 2-7. ETT in place, IO established.
1mg epinephrine administered via IO at 0446. Protocol activated.
Asystole remains. PEA noted in brief intervals. No other changes.
Autopulse activated to assist CPR. Compressions at appropriate depth and rate.
Final check: ETT at 24cm. Bougie passed through cords with assistance.
Second dose of epinephrine at 0453. No improvement in rhythm.
Asystole continues. PEA still not sustained. Classic indications of arrest.
Third dose of epinephrine at 0457. Same response as before.
Requesting [COMPANY_NAME] directives for resuscitation orders.
[COMPANY_NAME] approves to cease resuscitation at 0508.
Autopulse and BVM in place. No transport needed-patient is deceased.
Both [COMPANY_NAME] and Dr. Taylor confirmed cease of resuscitation.
Initial findings included unresponsiveness, no breathing, and asystole.
Three rounds of epinephrine given at tibial IO site. All per protocol.
IO line placed in tibia after failed humeral attempt. Confirmed by draw and flush.
Intubation performed with McGrath and bougie confirmation. Capnography used.
Complete documentation of interventions and time-stamps. All per standing orders.
No signs of drug use or trauma observed. No contraindications for continued CPR.
GCS of 3 found upon arrival, with no improvement noted despite interventions.
No spontaneous respiration. No central pulse detected initially.
7.5 mm endotracheal tube placed at 24 cm from the lips.
ETT secured with a tube holder. Breath delivered every 6 seconds.
First IO access failed at the humeral site but succeeded at the tibial site.
First epinephrine administered at 04:46 from tibial IO site.
Second dose of epinephrine at 04:53. No rhythm change with initial dose.
Intubation confirmed with epiglottic visualization and waveform capnography.
ETT advancement confirmed via tracheal ring clicks and bougie hold-up.
All medications and interventions were given in direct compliance with Standing Orders.
Asystole remained the leading rhythm for duration of care.
Time of arrest was estimated to be 5-10 mins before our arrival.
Epi administered at 04:57, before requesting [COMPANY_NAME] for directives.
Obstacles included rapid deterioration and unresponsive state upon arrival.
No steps were skipped or protocol deviations occurred during any phase of care.
Patient exhibited no signs of injury, prior medication adherence until onset.
Character of initial seizures was reportedly non-grand mal but included verbal disorientation.
No trauma inhaled, swallowed, or exposed during scene or transport.
No lytes imbalances or EKG complexes aside from asystole and PEA.
Repeat measurements all maintained asystolic, with no return to viable rhythm.
Autopulse device used after initial hands-on CPR performed post arrival.
No contraindications for CPR or intubation found pre or post intervention.
Respiration confirmed at 0 via manual and instrumental means.
Continuing protocols due to GCS 3 and minimal medical history provided.
Epinephrine effectively given via IO using 1:10,000 concentration per standard.
All medical records, protocol sheets, time stamped and filed accordingly.
Verbal reports given to [COMPANY_NAME] as-well as direct confirmation with on-scene physician.
Further transport deemed unnecessary due to lack of improvement.
All actions taken were consistent with training, education, and regional procedure guidelines.
Final check performed on airway and administered medications.
End of EMT involvement. CPR and interventions ceased at 05:08.
Reassessed status at 05:06. Asystole confirmed, no chest compressions obtained.
No return of spontaneous circulation. No pulse, no respirations.
Asystole continues to be the dominant rhythm.
No reversal or deterioration beyond initial condition noted.
All interventions per protocol, no further action needed.
No additional medications or procedures required per deployment crew.
Rao Membrane had been sealed, and circuit switched properly.
No new signs or symptoms beyond initial reported event.
No updated information was available from family or roommate.
Patient packaging wrapped and covered post-cessation of interventions.
Terminal events noted for medical records and [COMPANY_NAME] follow-up.
No urgent transport needed due to established directives.
Scene secured with no additional calls required.
Patient remains non-responsive, with irreversible condition confirmed.
Final documentation posted witte and without error.
Return to base discussed upon completion of clinical end of care.
Interventions paused and stoppage of compresions per [COMPANY_NAME] orders.
Meds not modified, and with no additional drugs on scene per standing orders.
Custodial care followed at ending time on scene with no decline further.
ECG finds remain consistent throughout the time of intervention.
Autopulse device deactivated at time of gunfire for discontinuation.
Nearest available hospital reviewed and not sought due to COD confirmation.
Folders cleaned to reflect chronological and unaltered clinical paper trail.
Legal forms filed at-will and reported to on-duty supervisor.
Photo of site and human form documented for administrative follow-up.
No family manifestations or audible complaints on scene.
All personnel remain calm and clinical at end of call.
Environmental no-hazards, no dosimeters activated, no body fluids on personnel.
Equipment Sanitized and cleaned; ETT removed for debris.
BVM breached for inspection and found acceptable for future use.
12-lead stripped and archived in ECG files awaiting processing.
IO site dressed and covered for future follow-up.
L V messages communicated, and assurance given regarding patient welfare.
Radio silence and quiet kept between twenty and twenty-two to avoid minutes lost.
All technical terms used during procedure were aligned per EMT and RFD joint instructions.
Value of breath-hold, respiration zero at anytime confirmed.
All pressure points trigged were parallel or mirrored from protocols.
Scene conclusion done at time consistent with on-scene coordination.
No additional coordination needed, legal line crossed.
Resumption of routine scheduled denied for further dispatches.
End of .arb lift protocols applied. No weights lifted, no findings disturbed.
Final EMT crew ready for debrief at default station prior to return.
Transport vehicle remains secure and ready for the next shift.
All prereqs fulfilled per proforma, and notes are digitally archived.
All IO lines non-critical at time of scene, and hardware sealed.
End of care marking as a temporary occurrence, not on-going.
Non-hospital managed cases tagged for PM interpretation.
No demand existed for in-field or emergent ICU referrals.
Ambulance parked, no smell or traffic hazards noted.
